paper_title: 14 C Measurements and Study Site Description

methods.description: This method describes the process of extracting and preparing calcite samples from a stalagmite for radiocarbon (14 C) analysis using Accelerator Mass Spectrometry (AMS). The purpose is to analyze 14 C activity over specific depth ranges to cover the bomb pulse and to obtain radiocarbon ages.

steps.description: Fifteen calcite cubes weighing w100 mg were extracted (using a carbide dental burr fitted to an air drill) every w5 mm along the central growth axis of LR06-B1 between 345 and 422 mm (i.e. 2.4e2.8 ka) from the top of the stalagmite.
steps.searchable_summary: extract calcite cubes from stalagmite using dental burr

steps.description: 21 samples, micromilled as calcite powders and weighing w3e5 mg, were extracted at 0.15e0.45 mm increments along the growth axis from the top of the stalagmite down to a depth of 6.15 mm.
Calcite powders and chunks were dissolved in 2 mL 85% H3PO4, producing CO2 gas that was graphitized using excess H2 over an Fe catalyst.
steps.searchable_summary: dissolve calcite in H3PO4 and graphitize CO2

steps.description: The graphite was then rear-pressed in an aluminium cathode for AMS measurement.
14 C measurements were conducted on the 2MV HVEE 'STAR' AMS in the Institute for Environmental Research, Australian Nuclear Science and Technology Organisation (ANSTO).
Calcite 14 C measurements were normalized to oxalic acid standards (HOxI and HOxII), which were interspersed for each run.
steps.searchable_summary: normalize 14 C measurements to oxalic acid standards

steps.description: 14 C measurements were also blank-corrected using the IAEA C1 marble procedural blank and assessed for machine background using spectroscopic-grade powdered graphite from Union Carbide Co.
steps.searchable_summary: blank-correct 14 C measurements and assess machine background
